params.text:
Communication that connects

Good communication is the heart of supporting someone with a mental health condition or dementia. It is about far more than words — your tone, pace, face and body all send messages. When words become difficult, these become even more important.

  • Give time: slow down, pause, and allow the person space to respond without pressure.
  • Keep it simple: short sentences and one idea at a time are easier to follow.
  • Get down to eye level: approach calmly from the front and make gentle eye contact.
  • Watch non-verbal cues: a frown, a turned shoulder or a smile tells you a great deal.

Listening and reassuring

Listening well shows respect and builds trust. Let the person lead where you can, and value what they are trying to express even if the words are muddled.

  • Listen actively: nod, reflect back what you hear, and show you are interested.
  • Avoid arguing: if someone believes something that is not accurate, focus on the feeling behind it rather than correcting the facts.
  • Reduce distractions: turn down background noise so the person can concentrate.
  • Offer reassurance: a calm voice and a warm manner can ease worry when words cannot.

Communication is a two-way relationship. When you adapt to the person — their pace, their preferred name, their good time of day — you help them stay connected, understood and valued. If communication needs change, report it so the care plan stays up to date.

params.question: A person living with dementia insists it is time to collect her children from school, though they are grown adults. What is the most person-centred response?

answers.text: Respond calmly to the feeling behind her words, offering reassurance rather than correcting the facts.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. Acknowledging the emotion and reassuring her respects her reality and reduces distress.

answers.text: Explain firmly that her children are adults now and there is no school to go to.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Bluntly correcting the facts can cause distress and confusion. Focus on the feeling, not the accuracy.

answers.text: Ignore what she says and carry on with the task at hand.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Ignoring the person dismisses a real feeling. She needs acknowledgement and reassurance.

answers.text: Tell her she is confused because of her dementia.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: This is neither kind nor helpful. Connect with the emotion behind her words instead.
